title: Tamerlane - Early Version
content:
I.

آ آ آ آ I have sent for thee, holy friar;1
آ آ آ آ But ï؟½twas not with the drunken hope,
آ آ آ آ Which is but agony of desire
آ آ آ آ To shun the fate, with which to cope
آ آ آ آ Is more than crime may dare to dream,
آ آ آ آ That I have callï؟½d thee at this hour:
آ آ آ آ Such father is not my theme
آ آ آ آ Nor am I mad, to deem that power
آ آ آ آ Of earth may shrive me of the sin
آ آ آ آ Unearthly pride hath revellï؟½d in
آ آ آ آ I would not call thee fool, old man,
آ آ آ آ But hope is not a gift of thine;
آ آ آ آ If I can hope (O God! I can)
آ آ آ آ It falls from an eternal shrine.

II.

آ آ آ آ The gay wall of this gaudy tower
آ آ آ آ Grows dim around me, death is near.
آ آ آ آ I had not thought, until this hour
آ آ آ آ When passing from the earth, that ear
آ آ آ آ Of any, were it not the shade
آ آ آ آ Of one whom in life I made
آ آ آ آ All mystery but a simple name,
آ آ آ آ Might know the secret of a spirit
آ آ آ آ Bowï؟½d down in sorrow, and in shame.
آ آ آ آ Shame saidï؟½st thou?

آ آ آ آ Aye I did inherit
آ آ آ آ That hatred portion, with the fame,
آ آ آ آ The worldly glory, which has shown
آ آ آ آ A demon-light around my throne,
آ آ آ آ Scorching my searï؟½d heart with a pain
آ آ آ آ Not Hell shall make me fear again.

III.

آ آ آ آ I have not always been as now
آ آ آ آ The feverï؟½d diadem on my brow
آ آ آ آ I claimï؟½d and won usurpingly
آ آ آ آ Aye, the same heritage hath givï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Rome to the Cï؟½sar, this to me;
آ آ آ آ The heirdom of a kingly mind,
آ آ آ آ And a proud spirit, which hath strivï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Triumphantly with human kind.

آ آ آ آ In mountain air I first drew life;
آ آ آ آ The mists of the Taglay have shed 2
آ آ آ آ Nightly their dews on my young head;
آ آ آ آ And my brain drank their venom then,
آ آ آ آ When after day of perilous strife
آ آ آ آ With chamois, I would seize his den
آ آ آ آ And slumber, in my pride of power,
آ آ آ آ The infant monarch of the hour,
آ آ آ آ For, with the mountain dew by night,
آ آ آ آ My soul imbibï؟½d unhallowï؟½d feeling;
آ آ آ آ And I would feel its essence stealing
آ آ آ آ In dreams upon me, while the light
آ آ آ آ Flashing from cloud that hoverï؟½d oï؟½er,
آ آ آ آ Would seem to my half closing eye
آ آ آ آ The pageantry of monarchy!
آ آ آ آ And the deep thunderï؟½s echoing roar
آ آ آ آ Came hurriedly upon me, telling
آ آ آ آ Of war, and tumult, where my voice
آ آ آ آ My own voice, silly child! was swelling
آ آ آ آ (O how would my wild heart rejoice
آ آ آ آ And leap within me at the cry)
آ آ آ آ The battle-cry of victory!
آ آ آ آ 
IV.

آ آ آ آ The rain came down upon my head
آ آ آ آ But barely shelterï؟½d, and the wind
آ آ آ آ Passï؟½d quickly oï؟½er me, but my mind
آ آ آ آ Was madï؟½ning, for ï؟½twas man that shed
آ آ آ آ Laurels upon me, and the rush,
آ آ آ آ The torrent of the chilly air
آ آ آ آ Gurgled in my pleasï؟½d ear the crash
آ آ آ آ Of empires, with the captiveï؟½s prayer,
آ آ آ آ The hum of suitors, the mixï؟½d tone
آ آ آ آ Of flattï؟½ry round a sovï؟½reignï؟½s throne.

آ آ آ آ The storm had ceasï؟½d, and I awoke,
آ آ آ آ Its spirit cradled me to sleep,
آ آ آ آ And as it passï؟½d me by, there broke
آ آ آ آ Strange light upon me, thoï؟½ it were
آ آ آ آ My soul in mystery to sleep:
آ آ آ آ For I was not as I had been;
آ آ آ آ The child of Nature, without care,
آ آ آ آ Or thought, save of the passing scene.

V.

آ آ آ آ My passions, from that hapless hour,
آ آ آ آ Usurpï؟½d a tyranny, which men
آ آ آ آ Have deemï؟½d, since I have reachï؟½d to power
آ آ آ آ My innate nature, be it so:
آ آ آ آ But, father, there livï؟½d one who, then,
آ آ آ آ Then, in my boyhood, when their fire
آ آ آ آ Burnï؟½d with a still intenser glow;
آ آ آ آ (For passion must with youth expire)
آ آ آ آ Evï؟½n then, who deemï؟½d this iron heart
آ آ آ آ In womanï؟½s weakness had a part.

آ آ آ آ I have no words, alas! to tell
آ آ آ آ The lovliness of loving well!
آ آ آ آ Nor would I dare attempt to trace
آ آ آ آ The breathing beauty of a face,
آ آ آ آ Which evï؟½n to my impassionï؟½d mind,
آ آ آ آ Leaves not its memory behind.
آ آ آ آ In spring of life have ye neï؟½er dwelt
آ آ آ آ Some object of delight upon,
آ آ آ آ With steadfast eye, till ye have felt
آ آ آ آ The earth reel, and the vision gone?
آ آ آ آ And I have held to memï؟½ryï؟½s eye
آ آ آ آ One object, and but one, until
آ آ آ آ Its very form hath passï؟½d me by,
آ آ آ آ But left its influence with me still.

VI.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis not to thee that I should name,
آ آ آ آ Thou canï؟½st not, wouldï؟½st not dare to think
آ آ آ آ The magic empire of a flame
آ آ آ آ Which evï؟½n upon this perilous brink
آ آ آ آ Hath fixï؟½d my soul, thoï؟½ unforgivï؟½n
آ آ آ آ By what it lost for passion, Heavï؟½n.
آ آ آ آ I lovï؟½d, and O, how tenderly!
آ آ آ آ Yes! she worthy of all love!
آ آ آ آ Such as in infancy was mine
آ آ آ آ Thoï؟½ then its passion could not be:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas such as angel minds above
آ آ آ آ Might envy, her young heart the shrine
آ آ آ آ On which my evï؟½ry hope and thought
آ آ آ آ Were incense, then a goodly gift,
آ آ آ آ For they were childish, without sin,
آ آ آ آ Pure as her young examples taught;
آ آ آ آ Why did I leave it and adrift,
آ آ آ آ Trust to the fickle star within?

VII.

آ آ آ آ We grew in age, and love together,
آ آ آ آ Roaming the forest and the wild;
آ آ آ آ My breast her shield in wintry weather,
آ آ آ آ And when the friendly sunshine smilï؟½d
آ آ آ آ And she would mark the opï؟½ning skies,
آ آ آ آ I saw no Heavï؟½n, but in her eyes,
آ آ آ آ Evï؟½n childhood knows the human heart;
آ آ آ آ For when, in sunshine and in smiles,
آ آ آ آ From all our little cares apart,
آ آ آ آ Laughing at her half silly wiles,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d throw me on her throbbing breast,
آ آ آ آ And pour my spirit out in tears,
آ آ آ آ Sheï؟½d look up in my wilderï؟½d eye,
آ آ آ آ There was no need to speak the rest,
آ آ آ آ No need to quiet her kind fears,
آ آ آ آ She did not ask the reason why.

آ آ آ آ The hallowï؟½d memï؟½ry of those years
آ آ آ آ Comes oï؟½er me in these lonely hours,
آ آ آ آ And, with sweet lovliness, appears
آ آ آ آ As perfume of strange summer flowï؟½rs;
آ آ آ آ Of flowï؟½rs which we have known before
آ آ آ آ In infancy, which seen, recall
آ آ آ آ To mind, not flowï؟½rs alone, but more
آ آ آ آ Our earthly life, and love, and all.

VIII.

آ آ آ آ Yes! she was worthy of all love!
آ آ آ آ Evï؟½n such as from thï؟½ accursed time
آ آ آ آ My spirit with the tempest strove,
آ آ آ آ When on the mountain peak alone,
آ آ آ آ Ambition lent it a new tone,
آ آ آ آ And bade it first to dream of crime,
آ آ آ آ My phrenzy to her bosom taught:
آ آ آ آ We still were young: no purer thought
آ آ آ آ Dwell in a seraphï؟½s breast than thine; 3
آ آ آ آ For passionate love is still divine:
آ آ آ آ I lovï؟½d her as an angel might
آ آ آ آ With ray of the all living light
آ آ آ آ Which blazes upon Edisï؟½ shrine. 4
آ آ آ آ It is not surely sin to name,
آ آ آ آ With such as mine, that mystic flame,
آ آ آ آ I had no being but in thee!
آ آ آ آ The world with all its train of bright
آ آ آ آ And happy beauty (for to me
آ آ آ آ All was an undefinï؟½d delight)
آ آ آ آ The world, its joy, its share of pain
آ آ آ آ Which I felt not, its bodied forms
آ آ آ آ Of varied being, which contain
آ آ آ آ The bodiless spirits of the storms,
آ آ آ آ The sunshine, and the calm, the ideal
آ آ آ آ And fleeting vanities of dreams,
آ آ آ آ Fearfully beautiful! the real
آ آ آ آ Nothings of mid-day waking life,
آ آ آ آ Of an enchanted life, which seems,
آ آ آ آ Now as I look back, the strife
آ آ آ آ Of some ill demon, with a power
آ آ آ آ Which left me in an evil hour,
آ آ آ آ All that I felt, or saw, or thought,
آ آ آ آ Crowding, confused became
آ آ آ آ (With thine unearthly beauty fraught)
آ آ آ آ Thou, and the nothing of a name.

IX.

آ آ آ آ The passionate spirit which hath known,
آ آ آ آ And deeply felt the silent tone
آ آ آ آ Of its own self supremacy,
آ آ آ آ (I speak thus openly to thee,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twere folly now to veil a thought
آ آ آ آ With which this aching, breast is fraught)
آ آ آ آ The soul which feels its innate right,
آ آ آ آ The mystic empire and high power
آ آ آ آ Givï؟½n by the energetic might
آ آ آ آ Of Genius, at its natal hour;
آ آ آ آ Which knows (believe me at this time,
آ آ آ آ When falsehood were a ten-fold crime,
آ آ آ آ There is a power in the high spirit
آ آ آ آ To know the fate it will inherit)
آ آ آ آ The soul, which knows such power, will still
آ آ آ آ Find Pride the ruler of its will.

آ آ آ آ Yes! I was proud, and ye who know
آ آ آ آ The magic of that meaning word,
آ آ آ آ So oft perverted, will bestow
آ آ آ آ Your scorn, perhaps, when ye have heard
آ آ آ آ That the proud spirit had been broken,
آ آ آ آ The proud heart burst in agony
آ آ آ آ At one upbraiding word or token
آ آ آ آ Of her that heartï؟½s idolatry,
آ آ آ آ I was ambitious, have ye known
آ آ آ آ Its fiery passion? ye have not,
آ آ آ آ A cottager, I markï؟½d a throne
آ آ آ آ Of half the world, as all my own,
آ آ آ آ And murmurï؟½d at such lowly lot!
آ آ آ آ But it had passï؟½d me as a dream
آ آ آ آ Which, of light step, flies with the dew,
آ آ آ آ That kindling thought, did not the beam
آ آ آ آ Of Beauty, which did guide it through
آ آ آ آ The livelong summer day, oppress
آ آ آ آ My mind with double loveliness
آ آ آ آ 
X.

آ آ آ آ We walkï؟½d together on the crown
آ آ آ آ Of a high mountain, which lookï؟½d down
آ آ آ آ Afar from its proud natural towers
آ آ آ آ Of rock and forest, on the hills,
آ آ آ آ The dwindled hills, whence amid bowers
آ آ آ آ Her own fair hand had rearï؟½d around,
آ آ آ آ Gushï؟½d shoutingly a thousand rills,
آ آ آ آ Which as it were, in fairy bound
آ آ آ آ Embracï؟½d two hamlets, those our own,
آ آ آ آ Peacefully happy, yet alone,
آ آ آ آ .آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  .آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  .آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  .آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  .
آ آ آ آ I spoke to her of power and pride,
آ آ آ آ But mystically, in such guise,
آ آ آ آ That she might deem it naught beside
آ آ آ آ The momentï؟½s converse, in her eyes
آ آ آ آ I read (perhaps too carelessly)
آ آ آ آ A mingled feeling with my own;
آ آ آ آ The flush on her bright cheek, to me,
آ آ آ آ Seemï؟½d to become a queenly throne
آ آ آ آ Too well, that I should let it be
آ آ آ آ A light in the dark wild, alone.

XI.

آ آ آ آ There, in that hour, a thought came oï؟½er
آ آ آ آ My mind, it had not known before,
آ آ آ آ To leave her while we both were young,
آ آ آ آ To follow my high fate among
آ آ آ آ The strife of nations, and redeem
آ آ آ آ The idle words, which, as a dream
آ آ آ آ Now sounded to her heedless ear,
آ آ آ آ I held no doubt, I knew no fear
آ آ آ آ Of peril in my wild career;
آ آ آ آ To gain an empire, and throw down
آ آ آ آ As nuptial dowry, a queenï؟½s crown,
آ آ آ آ The only feeling which possest,
آ آ آ آ With her own image, my fond breast,
آ آ آ آ Who, that had known the secret thought
آ آ آ آ Of a young peasantï؟½s bosom then,
آ آ آ آ Had deemï؟½d him, in compassion, aught
آ آ آ آ But one, whom phantasy had led
آ آ آ آ Astray from reason, Among men
آ آ آ آ Ambition is chainï؟½d down, nor fed
آ آ آ آ (As in the desert, where the grand,
آ آ آ آ The wild, the beautiful, conspire
آ آ آ آ With their own breath to fan its fire)
آ آ آ آ With thoughts such feeling can command;
آ آ آ آ Uncheckï؟½d by sarcasm, and scorn
آ آ آ آ Of those, who hardly will conceive
آ آ آ آ That any should become ï؟½great,ï؟½ born 5
آ آ آ آ In their own sphere, will not believe
آ آ آ آ That they shall stoop in life to one
آ آ آ آ Whom daily they are wont to see
آ آ آ آ Familiarly, whom Fortuneï؟½s sun
آ آ آ آ Hath neï؟½er shone dazzlingly upon
آ آ آ آ Lowly, and of their own degree,

XII.

آ آ آ آ I picturï؟½d to my fancyï؟½s eye
آ آ آ آ Her silent, deep astonishment,
آ آ آ آ When, a few fleeting years gone by,
آ آ آ آ (For short the time my high hope lent
آ آ آ آ To its most desperate intent,)
آ آ آ آ She might recall in him, whom Fame
آ آ آ آ Had gilded with a conquererï؟½s name,
آ آ آ آ (With glory, such as might inspire
آ آ آ آ Perforce, a passing thought of one,
آ آ آ آ Whom she had deemï؟½d in his own fire
آ آ آ آ Witherï؟½d and blasted; who had gone
آ آ آ آ A traitor, violate of the truth
آ آ آ آ So plighted in his early youth,)
آ آ آ آ Her own Alexis, who should plight 6
آ آ آ آ The love he plighted then, again,
آ آ آ آ And raise his infancyï؟½s delight,
آ آ آ آ The bride and queen of Tamerlane,

XIII.

آ آ آ آ One noon of a bright summerï؟½s day
آ آ آ آ I passï؟½d from out the matted bowï؟½r
آ آ آ آ Where in a deep, still slumber lay
آ آ آ آ My Ada. In that peaceful hour,
آ آ آ آ A silent gaze was my farewell.
آ آ آ آ I had no other solace, then
آ آ آ آ Tï؟½awake her, and a falsehood tell
آ آ آ آ Of a feignï؟½d journey, were again
آ آ آ آ To trust the weakness of my heart
آ آ آ آ To her soft thrilling voice: To part
آ آ آ آ Thus, haply, while in sleep she dreamï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Of long delight, nor yet had deemï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Awake, that I had held a thought
آ آ آ آ Of parting, were with madness fraught;
آ آ آ آ I knew not womanï؟½s heart, alas!
آ آ آ آ Thoï؟½ lovï؟½d, and loving, let it pass.

XIV.

آ آ آ آ I went from out the matted bowï؟½r,
آ آ آ آ And hurried madly on my way:
آ آ آ آ And felt, with evï؟½ry flying hour,
آ آ آ آ That bore me from my home, more gay;
آ آ آ آ There is of earth an agony
آ آ آ آ Which, ideal, still may be
آ آ آ آ The worst ill of mortality,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis bliss, in its own reality,
آ آ آ آ Too real, to his breast who lives
آ آ آ آ Not within himself but gives
آ آ آ آ A portion of his willing soul
آ آ آ آ To God, and to the great whole,
آ آ آ آ To him, whose loving spirit will dwell
آ آ آ آ With Nature, in her wild paths; tell
آ آ آ آ Of her wondï؟½rous ways, and telling bless
آ آ آ آ Her overpowï؟½ring loveliness!
آ آ آ آ A more than agony to him
آ آ آ آ Whose failing sight will grow dim
آ آ آ آ With its own living gaze upon
آ آ آ آ That loveliness around: the sun,
آ آ آ آ The blue sky, the misty light
آ آ آ آ Of the pale cloud therein, whose hue
آ آ آ آ Is grace to its heavï؟½nly bed of blue;
آ آ آ آ Dim! thoï؟½ looking on all bright!
آ آ آ آ O God! when the thoughts that may not pass
آ آ آ آ Will burst upon him, and alas!
آ آ آ آ For the flight on Earth to Fancy givï؟½n,
آ آ آ آ There are no words, unless of Heavï؟½n.

XV.

آ آ آ آ Look ï؟½round thee now on Samarcand, 7
آ آ آ آ Is she not queen of earth? her pride
آ آ آ آ Above all cities? in her hand
آ آ آ آ Their destinies? with all beside
آ آ آ آ Of glory, which the world hath known?
آ آ آ آ Stands she not proudly and alone?
آ آ آ آ And who her sovï؟½reign? Timur he 8
آ آ آ آ Whom thï؟½ astonishï؟½d earth hath seen,
آ آ آ آ With victory, on victory,
آ آ آ آ Redoubling age! and more, I ween,
آ آ آ آ The Zinghisï؟½ yet re-echoing fame. 9
آ آ آ آ And now what has he? what! a name.
آ آ آ آ The sound of revelry by night
آ آ آ آ Comes oï؟½er me, with the mingled voice
آ آ آ آ Of many with a breast as light,
آ آ آ آ As if ï؟½twere not the dying hour
آ آ آ آ Of one, in whom they did rejoice,
آ آ آ آ As in a leader, haply, Power
آ آ آ آ Its venom secretly imparts;
آ آ آ آ Nothing have I with human hearts.

XVI.

آ آ آ آ When Fortune markï؟½d me for her own,
آ آ آ آ And my proud hopes had reachï؟½d a throne
آ آ آ آ (It boots me not, good friar, to tell
آ آ آ آ A tale the world but knows too well,
آ آ آ آ How by what hidden deeds of might,
آ آ آ آ I clamberï؟½d to the tottering height,)
آ آ آ آ I still was young; and well I ween
آ آ آ آ My spirit what it eï؟½er had been.
آ آ آ آ My eyes were still on pomp and power,
آ آ آ آ My wilderï؟½d heart was far away,
آ آ آ آ In vallies of the wild Taglay,
آ آ آ آ In mine own Adaï؟½s matted bowï؟½r.
آ آ آ آ I dwelt not long in Samarcand
آ آ آ آ Ere, in a peasantï؟½s lowly guise,
آ آ آ آ I sought my long-abandonï؟½d land,
آ آ آ آ By sunset did its mountains rise
آ آ آ آ In dusky grandeur to my eyes:
آ آ آ آ But as I wanderï؟½d on the way
آ آ آ آ My heart sunk with the sunï؟½s ray.
آ آ آ آ To him, who still would gaze upon
آ آ آ آ The glory of the summer sun,
آ آ آ آ There comes, when that sun will from him part,
آ آ آ آ A sullen hopelessness of heart.
آ آ آ آ That soul will hate the evï؟½ning mist
آ آ آ آ So often lovely, and will lisp
آ آ آ آ To the sound of the coming darkness (known
آ آ آ آ To those whose spirits harkï؟½n) as one 10
آ آ آ آ Who in a dream of night would fly
آ آ آ آ But cannot from a danger nigh.
آ آ آ آ What though the moon, the silvery moon
آ آ آ آ Shine on his path, in her high noon;
آ آ آ آ Her smile is chilly, and her beam
آ آ آ آ In that time of dreariness will seem
آ آ آ آ As the portrait of one after death;
آ آ آ آ A likeness taken when the breath
آ آ آ آ Of young life, and the fire oï؟½ the eye
آ آ آ آ Had lately been but had passï؟½d by.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis thus when the lovely summer sun
آ آ آ آ Of our boyhood, his course hath run:
آ آ آ آ For all we live to know, is known;
آ آ آ آ And all we seek to keep, hath flown;
آ آ آ آ With the noon-day beauty, which is all.
آ آ آ آ Let life, then, as the day-flowï؟½r, fall,
آ آ آ آ The trancient, passionate day-flowï؟½r, 11
آ آ آ آ Withering at the evï؟½ning hour.

XVII.

آ آ آ آ I reachï؟½d my home, my home no more,
آ آ آ آ For all was flown that made it so,
آ آ آ آ I passï؟½d from out its mossy door,
آ آ آ آ In vacant idleness of woe.
آ آ آ آ There met me on its threshold stone
آ آ آ آ A mountain hunter, I had known
آ آ آ آ In childhood but he knew me not.
آ آ آ آ Something he spoke of the old cot:
آ آ آ آ It had seen better days, he said;
آ آ آ آ There rose a fountain once, and there
آ آ آ آ Full many a fair flowï؟½r raisï؟½d its head:
آ آ آ آ But she who rearï؟½d them was long dead,
آ آ آ آ And in such follies had no part,
آ آ آ آ What was there left me now? despair,
آ آ آ آ A kingdom for a broken heart.
meaning:
"Tamerlane - Early Version" is a quintessential example of Edgar Allan Poe's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
